paragraphs.text: C Ref

We are encouraged by the European Commission's renewed commitment to protecting all consumers online, levelling the playing-field and simplifying the regulatory regime, and, as such, welcome this opportunity to engage with the Commission on its public consultation on a Digital Fairness Act (DFA).

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Introduction

paragraphs.text: TikTok's response to this consultation is divided across both this paper and our responses to the questionnaire in light of the way in which the consultation is structured, with narrative responses only available when selecting an option that agrees further intervention (regulatory or non-regulatory) or enforcement is required. We therefore urge the Commission to review them as a package and, in its analysis of the responses more broadly, take into consideration the limitations stemming from the format of the consultation.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Introduction

paragraphs.text: This paper addresses the issues raised from the perspective of three main themes: (i) the protection of minors online and digital well-being; (ii) the importance of consumer-centric products and services; and (iii) boosting the protection of European consumers through simplification, harmonisation and enforcement.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Introduction

paragraphs.text: While this paper and our responses to the questionnaire are primarily focused on the substance of this public consultation, there are several broader, thematic observations and related points that TikTok wishes to bring to the attention of the Commission, as well as policy-makers more broadly, in this context.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Introduction

paragraphs.text: TikTok is truly supportive of effective regulation that sets a level-playing-field and aligns with our strong focus on user safety and empowerment, whilst also allowing room for innovation and growth in the European economy. Our approach is grounded in a strong willingness to engage constructively with policy-makers, regulators, and civil society organisations across Europe to design frameworks that are fit for purpose, future proof and enforceable.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
High-level comments

paragraphs.text: With this in mind, we would strongly urge the Commission to:

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
High-level comments

paragraphs.text: Prioritise strengthening the existing legislative framework through guidance and more effective, and centralised enforcement.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
High-level comments

paragraphs.text: Where legislation has recently been introduced, time should be given for these frameworks to embed, so their impact can be properly assessed. Only where clear, evidenced gaps remain and these cannot already be addressed through guidance or enforcement mechanisms, should targeted legislative interventions be considered. TikTok considers that there is only a very limited need for new regulatory intervention;

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
High-level comments

paragraphs.text: Ensure any such intervention effectively resolves such identified gaps, in an appropriate, proportionate, risk and principles-based, trader/service-agnostic and future-proof manner, whilst delivering on the overriding objectives of bringing clarity and simplification, levelling the playing-field and facilitating more effective, consistent, enforcement;

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
High-level comments

paragraphs.text: Maintain the horizontal nature of the EU's consumer acquis, which focuses on clearly identified consumer harms and not specific services/ technologies/ product features. It is crucial that there is a level-playing-field for all companies servicing European consumers regardless of size, sector and origin; and

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
High-level comments

evidence_quotes.quote: harmonisation and simplification at EU level.

paragraphs.text: Concretely avoid overlaps with or duplication of requirements under existing EU legislation, clearly address the interplay of existing rules covering the same or similar topics and assess opportunities for harmonisation and simplification at EU level.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
High-level comments

paragraphs.text: At TikTok, we are dedicated to ensuring our platform's design, features, and safeguards provide an age-appropriate experience for all users and empowers them to engage in a digital experience that inspires creativity and brings joy. This is particularly the case for our users aged 13-15 & 16-17 ("younger users"), as we recognise the particular risks they face online and embrace our responsibility to ensure their privacy, safety, and security on the platform. Broadly speaking, our approach is to protect, empower and learn.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

evidence_quotes.quote: age appropriate design.

paragraphs.text: We build robust safeguards to protect our younger users, particularly through age assurance and age appropriate design. The minimum age to use TikTok in the EU is 13 and we use a multi-layered approach to confirm someone's age or detect when they may not actually be the age they say they are. We enforce these requirements by utilising technology, human moderators and additional tools (e.g. reports from parents). In the last quarter, we removed over 25 million accounts globally that we suspected belonged to users under 13. It is important to stress that EU regulation, guidance and enforcement must be flexible enough to enable and encourage companies to find innovative solutions, such as using AI technologies, to uniquely digital challenges, such as preventing underage users from accessing their products/ services, particularly when solutions may touch on multiple legislative instruments and regulators.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

paragraphs.text: Understandably, there is a desire for a single, perfect solution to online age assurance, which is an industry-wide challenge. We are committed to sharing our expertise and learnings with policy-makers to find constructive solutions together, and we believe that a multi-layered approach is essential to protecting younger users and upholding

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

evidence_quotes.quote: safety-by-design principles.
the Commission's planned age verification app

paragraphs.text: safety-by-design principles. We're committed to keeping minors off our platform, providing younger users with age-appropriate experiences, and continuing to assess and implement a range of solutions. To that end, we have already started to explore whether the Commission's planned age verification app could be an effective additional tool for us.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

evidence_quotes.quote: Accounts of under 16s are private by default and their content is ineligible for recommendation.
the content they see and features they have access to will be age appropriate.
All under 18s have their screentime automatically set to 60 minutes and won't receive notifications after bedtime.
LIVE streaming, gifting and TikTok Shop, are only permitted for users that are over 18.

paragraphs.text: We have an industry-leading 50 safeguards built in by default for our younger users. These safeguards were developed in collaboration with child development specialists and consider these users' unique developmental needs, meaning those aged 13-15 experience TikTok differently to those aged 16-17, and the content they see and features they have access to will be age appropriate. Accounts of under 16s are private by default and their content is ineligible for recommendation. All under 18s have their screentime automatically set to 60 minutes and won't receive notifications after bedtime. Further, certain features or services, such as LIVE streaming, gifting and TikTok Shop, are only permitted for users that are over 18.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

paragraphs.text: We empower our younger users, as well as their families, to manage their own experiences through tools such as Family Pairing (allowing parents or guardians to link accounts and manage settings, such as blocking younger users from using apps during certain times (e.g. before bedtime)) and Restricted Mode (helping them limit exposure to content that may not be appropriate or comfortable for them). We strongly believe consumer empowerment is key to a thriving, competitive, digital economy and the use of such tools should be encouraged and facilitated by the Commission and by traders themselves, without being prescribed by legislation.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

evidence_quotes.quote: age assurance and minor safety

paragraphs.text: As we learn more, we do more. We iterate and implement policies and features on a regular basis, in consultation with younger users (such as via our Global Youth Council), parents, teachers, doctors and safety experts. For instance, in November 2024, we implemented changes to our platform to positively influence younger users' broader well-being, in line with research conducted by Internet Matters. We are also actively engaged in the Global Multistakeholder Dialogue on Age Assurance convened by the Centre for Information Policy Leadership (CIPL) and WeProtect Global Alliance. This dialogue aims to explore the complex challenges of age assurance and minor safety, whilst driving consensus across the sector.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

paragraphs.text: In her Political Guidelines 2024 - 2029, Commission President von der Leyen stated that "one of the greatest challenges of this decade is protecting the mental health of our children and young people - especially online" and that there is a need for "an open and evidence-based debate on this issue".

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

paragraphs.text: As a very large online entertainment platform, we have a clear responsibility towards our younger users and take concerns about their well-being and digital engagement incredibly seriously. We would like to stress, however, that there is a broader, societal responsibility for protecting minors online and and consider that, when assessing their well-being and digital engagement holistically, there are significantly fewer and less targeted protections available for consumers, especially minors, using services that are not regulated by the DSA despite risks identified in this consultation also existing in those contexts.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

evidence_quotes.quote: risks stemming from the design or functioning of their service

paragraphs.text: Under the DSA, Very Large Online Platforms (VLOPs), such as TikTok, are subject to extensive requirements, many of which are still only just bedding in. These include obligations to conduct systemic risk assessments and mitigate any risks stemming from the design or functioning of their service, including for minors and users' mental well-being. Not only are these risk assessments prepared annually, and audited, but critical risk impact assessments are conducted prior to launching new products or services that may carry the most material risks. TikTok has dedicated significant resources to ensure ongoing compliance with the DSA, among other laws. This helps us to further protect, empower and learn, and this model of ongoing engagement and open dialogue is one we would urge the Commission, as well as relevant regulators, to adopt more broadly across the digital economy.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

evidence_quotes.quote: persuasive design features that are aimed predominantly at engagement and that may lead to extensive use or overuse of the platform or problematic or compulsive behavioural habits

paragraphs.text: Relatedly, we welcome the recent adoption of the Commission's Guidelines on the online protection of minors under the DSA which inter alia provides guidance on "persuasive design features that are aimed predominantly at engagement and that may lead to extensive use or overuse of the platform or problematic or compulsive behavioural habits". We consider this terminology as more appropriate than that used in this consultation regarding 'addictive design features'. Scientific consensus on 'digital addiction' is still evolving and reliance on 'screentime' as evidence in this regard is overly simplistic (see here and here, for instance). We take concerns about well-being and digital engagement incredibly seriously and are leading the industry in well-being features which give our users control of their online experience (including their screentime), as well as support organisations in creating authoritative, engaging and uplifting mental health content.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

paragraphs.text: We would urge the Commission to ensure that the scope, scale and materiality of the risk in this area is given rigorous assessment and that any proposed mitigations are demonstrated to be appropriate, effective and proportionate to the risk. The Commission should take a considered, strategic, approach to adopting further interventions, and allow the time for interventions to become established, and their impact appropriately measured, before going further. There is considerable rhetoric on this topic as the digital ecosystem evolves and as we learn more about how consumers interact with digital services. A variety of different interventions are currently being proposed across regulatory frameworks and implementing multiple 'solutions' at once risks creating a disjointed, complex, and duplicative regime that is ultimately ineffective at achieving its aims.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

paragraphs.text: In light of the above, TikTok:

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

evidence_quotes.quote: persuasive design features that are aimed predominantly at engagement
extensive use or overuse of digital services or problematic or compulsive behavioural habits

paragraphs.text: Supports a general cross-industry requirement for all online services and traders employing "persuasive design features that are aimed predominantly at engagement" to appropriately assess the risk that they may lead to "extensive use or overuse of digital services or problematic or compulsive behavioural habits" and to take reasonable and proportionate steps to mitigate any material risks identified. This requirement should be proportionate to the nature of the service, features present, trader's size, user base (i.e. whether it is available to minors) and potential risks associated with the particular online service. The recently adopted Guidelines on Article 28 of the

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

evidence_quotes.quote: avoid blanket prohibitions on design features which may, depending on the context, pose no risks in this regard, including to minors.

paragraphs.text: DSA, which applies to all online platforms, could form a good reference point for the operation of this requirement in the context of potential risks to minors. It is important to emphasise that a flexible, proportionate and risk-based approach should be the focus, and that the Commission should avoid blanket prohibitions on design features which may, depending on the context, pose no risks in this regard, including to minors. With a view to avoiding duplicative/ overlapping requirements, any cross-industry obligation should not be applicable to VLOPs, who are already required to assess and mitigate such risks to all users under the DSA;

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

evidence_quotes.quote: assessing risks relating to mental well-being

paragraphs.text: Encourages the Commission to come forward with guidance under the DSA, as per Article 35(3), to assist VLOPs in carrying out their obligations under Articles 34 and 35, including assessing risks relating to mental well-being; and

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

paragraphs.text: Considers that the Commission's upcoming EU-wide inquiry should look at online services and traders generally, as well as broader societal factors. To limit the scope narrowly would be a missed opportunity. We appreciate the complexity of a broader exercise and stand ready to support the Commission, Member States and European Parliament's work. This inquiry should aim to understand the root causes of concerns, identify targeted actions and establish a roadmap for securing broader societal support and adoption.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 1: Protection of minors online and digital well-being

evidence_quotes.quote: Our personalised recommender system, the For You Feed (FYF), is at the heart of the TikTok experience.

paragraphs.text: From #BookTok to #FarmTok and beyond, more than 200 million people across Europe regularly come to TikTok to discover things they love, build communities, and express themselves. Our personalised recommender system, the For You Feed (FYF), is at the heart of the TikTok experience. We are transparent about the way in which the FYF works, we offer European users a variety of additional ways to enjoy TikTok (non-personalised feeds, STEM feed, Following, LIVE, Shop), and we empower users to control their own online experience (e.g. ability to refresh their FYF, manage the ads that they see, and set LIVE Gifting limits).

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 2: The importance of consumer-centric products and services

evidence_quotes.quote: personalised advertising
creator marketing

paragraphs.text: Every day, diverse, innovative and creative small and medium-sized businesses (SMEs) from all over Europe use TikTok to reach customers, build their brands, and grow. In 2023, SMEs on TikTok contributed an additional €4.8 billion to European economies, whilst supporting 51,100 jobs. Owners of the SMEs were almost three times more likely to be from a diverse background, and more likely to be under 30. Not only can businesses leverage personalised advertising and creator marketing, but SMEs, in particular, are well placed to use TikTok for organic growth, given its low barriers to entry and recommender system designed to allow users to discover lesser-known products, services and brands.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: personalisation in digital interactions

paragraphs.text: As its comments in the consultation questionnaire demonstrate, the Commission clearly recognises the benefits and importance of personalisation in digital interactions, not only to consumers but to the wider European economy.

paragraphs.text: growing digital economy that allows consumers to engage efficiently, effectively and in a low-cost manner.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 2: The importance of consumer-centric products and services

evidence_quotes.quote: When it comes to advertising based on profiling, online platforms are already prohibited from doing so to minors, as well as from using special categories of personal data.
When it comes to the concerns relating to certain aspects of personalisation raised in this consultation,

paragraphs.text: When it comes to the concerns relating to certain aspects of personalisation raised in this consultation, the EU already has a comprehensive legal framework in place (e.g. covering the types of data that can be used for personalised advertising, the targeting of certain users (e.g. minors) and providing users the ability to control their own experiences). This includes, inter alia, the GDPR, the ePrivacy Directive, and the UCPD. Online platforms, and designated VLOPs in particular, such as TikTok, are subject to additional, and auditable, regulatory requirements under the DSA, as well as required to conduct risk assessments. When it comes to advertising based on profiling, online platforms are already prohibited from doing so to minors, as well as from using special categories of personal data. For these reasons, TikTok does not consider that clear gaps in relation to personalisation have been identified.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 2: The importance of consumer-centric products and services

evidence_quotes.quote: more and more ways to shape their online experience
information to be provided in a way that is suitable for them

paragraphs.text: As highlighted above, in response to consumer demand, as well as regulatory requirements, online services are providing consumers with more and more ways to shape their online experience. However, for consumers to be empowered in practice, this requires information to be provided in a way that is suitable for them, as well as for the online service and the medium through which it is experienced.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 2: The importance of consumer-centric products and services

evidence_quotes.quote: 'dark patterns'

paragraphs.text: With this in mind, it is important to emphasise that, in an online context, and particularly for mobile devices - where space is, by definition, constrained - decisions have to be made regarding the form and manner in which information is presented. Online services are best placed to design their services in a consumer-centric way - giving consumers a seamless experience which empowers choice in the context of their specific service. Many of the decisions that have to be made, however, reflect specific legal requirements which are not always easily reconcilable. For instance, while TikTok very much welcomed the objectives of and engaged constructively with the Cookie Pledge initiative, which aimed inter alia to address consumer information fatigue over cookie choices, the process clearly demonstrated it is not possible to effectively achieve these objectives without aligning regulatory requirements, guidance and enforcement across different legislative acts. Similarly, while the existing flexible and principles-based frameworks relating to 'dark patterns' comprehensively address the practices raised by the Commission in this consultation, the hierarchy and interrelationship between the frameworks is unclear, particularly as there is no consistent definition of 'dark patterns' or, using the more appropriate term, 'harmful online choice architecture'. It is also worth pointing out that, as the CJEU has recently confirmed, the "average consumer" concept is already a dynamic concept, which can continually adapt to protect consumers, including online. In its Compass Banca judgment, the Court underlined this concept does not exclude "the possibility that an individual's decision-making capacity may be impaired by constraints, such as cognitive biases". In doing so, the Court affirmed that the concept of the "reasonably well-informed and reasonably observant and circumspect" consumer is flexible enough to consider practical realities and insights of behavioural research, such as regarding information overload or the way they engage with decision making in the modern digital world.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 2: The importance of consumer-centric products and services

paragraphs.text: When it comes to the information that needs to be provided to consumers, the EU's existing legal framework already establishes comprehensive rules as to what must be included (as well as what must not be omitted) and flexible principles regarding how and when that information must be presented.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Chapter 2: The importance of consumer-centric products and services

evidence_quotes.quote: Further, it comprehensively and expressly prohibits all forms of hidden digital advertising, setting out clear obligations in this regard for influencers and the brands they partner with.

TikTok invests significant resources in ensuring that any commercial content on the platform, including from influencers (or 'creators') and brands, is or can be clearly disclosed, in line with existing legal requirements.
For instance, we automatically label paid-for ads and make commercial content disclosure functionality available for brands and influencers creating content on our platform.

paragraphs.text: Further, it comprehensively and expressly prohibits all forms of hidden digital advertising, setting out clear obligations in this regard for influencers and the brands they partner with. Many of the challenges relating to influencer marketing that are raised in this consultation stem from the lack of awareness of (and therefore compliance by) the influencers and brands themselves, in addition to fragmented implementation and inconsistent enforcement at EU and national level. TikTok invests significant resources in ensuring that any commercial content on the platform, including from influencers (or 'creators') and brands, is or can be clearly disclosed, in line with existing legal requirements. For instance, we automatically label paid-for ads and make commercial content disclosure functionality available for brands and influencers creating content on our platform. Further, our terms, policies and Creator Academy provide information for creators, brands and agencies on our policies around regulated goods and commercial activities, which address concerns raised in the consultation about the promotion and sale of certain products/ services. All creators are also required to sign up to our Creator Code of Conduct, which inter alia covers off-platform activity. Resources like the Commission's Influencer Legal Hub can support broader awareness, and we would support further efforts in this regard, perhaps in the form of further guidance and/or possible EU-level recognition for industry initiatives and self-regulatory standards (such as the European Advertising Standards Alliance (EASA) AdEthics programme and the DiscloseMe tool).

paragraphs.text: Supports centralised cross-regulatory guidance on 'dark patterns' or 'harmful online choice architecture', which clarifies and explains the hierarchy and interrelationship between the different regimes, more clearly delineates between legitimate business practices and those that are 'harmful' and addresses the fundamental inconsistency in the use and definition of 'dark pattern' or 'harmful online choice architecture';

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: We welcome the fact that the Commission has launched a simplification agenda given the volume of digital legislation introduced in recent years, some of which are overlapping and, at times, conflicting. We consider this agenda as a genuine opportunity to enhance European competitiveness, by contributing to a regulatory environment that is easier for all market participants, including consumers, to navigate and one that supports rather than hinders compliance efforts. We encourage the Commission to take an ambitious approach overall to simplification and to introduce meaningful change for businesses who are eager to invest in Europe.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer marketing

paragraphs.text: A lack of harmonisation across EU consumer law, caused by the fragmented approach to national implementation, inconsistency of interpretation and the introduction of national laws addressing similar areas (e.g. influencer marketing), has given rise to a lack of legal certainty, is a significant burden for businesses who operate cross-border, and has a chilling effect on innovation. There are certain areas covered by this consultation where a harmonised approach would be beneficial, although in the majority of instances, TikTok considers this can be achieved through updated or new guidance, rather than legislative intervention.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
In light of the above, TikTok:

paragraphs.text: As is highlighted throughout TikTok's submission to this consultation, in particular our responses to the questionnaire, the EU has a comprehensive legal framework for addressing the concerns raised. The major challenge to the protection of consumers, clarifying interpretations and levelling the playing-field, however, is effective enforcement.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
In light of the above, TikTok:

evidence_quotes.quote: 'dark patterns'

paragraphs.text: The Commission should consider a centralised supervisory and enforcement authority, through a revision of the CPC Regulation, with the power to initiate and undertake investigations and oversee centralised enforcement where there is an alleged widespread infringement, as well as mediate cross-border outcomes. A clear enforcement strategy at EU level, including establishing annual priority areas of scrutiny, would support businesses with ongoing compliance efforts and allow them to prioritise resources to tackle concerns prior to enforcement actions being required, as well as allow national enforcers to align their actions with the broader EU objectives. This should be accompanied by a structure for greater collaboration, coordination and alignment with authorities responsible for enforcement under other legislative frameworks such as the DSA, GDPR and Data Act, and on cross-cutting topics (such as 'dark patterns'/'harmful online choice architecture').

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
In light of the above, TikTok:

paragraphs.text: TikTok would also support:

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
In light of the above, TikTok:

evidence_quotes.quote: collaboration with industry or specific traders to share concerns and seek resolution in a more constructive manner.
Formal enforcement should be a last resort;

paragraphs.text: Earlier, and more regular, collaboration with industry or specific traders to share concerns and seek resolution in a more constructive manner. Formal enforcement should be a last resort;

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
In light of the above, TikTok:

evidence_quotes.quote: Additional transparency during the enforcement process

paragraphs.text: Additional transparency during the enforcement process, particularly in relation to outcomes such as commitments, decisions or the closing of investigations, and more consistent 'follow-through' of decisions in respect of other traders engaged in similar activity. This will give all interested parties greater awareness of the principles established, simplify the regulatory landscape, increase legal certainty and level the playing-field;

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
In light of the above, TikTok:

paragraphs.text: Regular iteration of guidance, for example on an annual basis, to account for relevant enforcement developments;

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
In light of the above, TikTok:

evidence_quotes.quote: 'in-game virtual currencies'

paragraphs.text: A structured ongoing dialogue with relevant stakeholders, including industry and civil society, particularly when guidance is being developed. TikTok considers that the CPC key principles on 'in-game virtual currencies' are a good starting point in this regard but should be iterated and reframed in conjunction with industry to address existing concerns, for example, to mitigate negative user experience from information overload in high-volume, recurring, transactions and to ensure that consumer harm is being properly addressed whilst mitigating unnecessary restraints on businesses; and

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
In light of the above, TikTok:

evidence_quotes.quote: drip pricing

paragraphs.text: Continued and, where relevant, updated Commission-level sweeps, for instance on price transparency and drip pricing, with follow-up enforcement actions where necessary.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
In light of the above, TikTok:

paragraphs.text: With a view to a clear, simplified, harmonised and enforceable regulatory framework, we would also urge the Commission to avoid introducing new, vague concepts such as "fairness by design" or reversing the burden of proof.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
In light of the above, TikTok:

paragraphs.text: We are encouraged by the Commission's commitment to enhancing the protection of all consumers online, regardless the types of services they use or transactions they enter into, and are steadfast in our support for taking additional measures to protect the most vulnerable consumers, especially minors, in their digital interactions.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Conclusion

paragraphs.text: We believe the recommendations we have made above and in our questionnaire responses will ensure the continued development of consumer-centric digital products and services, level the playing-field for traders operating across the EU and contribute to a simplified and harmonised regulatory framework which can be effectively enforced. In the majority of areas covered by this consultation, this can be achieved through non-legislative action, most notably by more effectively, and consistently, enforcing the comprehensive existing rulebook. That said, we have identified a very limited number of areas where targeted interventions, including through guidance, could be considered.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Conclusion

paragraphs.text: We appreciate the opportunity to respond to this consultation, and are available for further follow-up.

section_path: TikTok's response to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Conclusion

paragraphs.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
